nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving, shallow tributaries, streams, and oxbow lakes with sandy or silty substrates, often with abundant leaf litter and driftwood.
nativeRange.biotope: Upper Rio Madeira basin; Rio Mamoré and Rio Guaporé (Iténez) drainages.

diet.notes: Naturally benthophagic — sifts sandy substrate for small organisms, detritus, plant matter, and seeds. In captivity generally accepts quality sinking pellets, frozen or live bloodworm, brine shrimp, daphnia, and white worms. Variety tends to support good color and conditioning.

imageCandidates.notes: Elma from Reykjavik; 2252x1503. Striking full-nuptial-colour male: gold-orange body, bright red-edged fins, extended dorsal/caudal filaments, black eye bar and midbody spot all crisp and vivid.

Corpse89; 4592x3056. Tack-sharp clean lateral view showing all diagnostic marks (eye bar, midbody spot, caudal-peduncle spot, red dorsal edge, orange belly) over sand; slightly pale but clearly in colour.
Ron Goodrich; 2592x1944. Sharp planted-tank shot showing red-margined fins, red caudal edges, dark eye bar and midbody blotch; natural biotope look for variety.

summary: The Bolivian Ram is a dwarf cichlid from Bolivia and Brazil, generally considered a more forgiving entry point into cichlid-keeping than its close relative the German Blue Ram. It tends to tolerate a wider range of water conditions and is widely regarded as a peaceful, pair-bonding species with attentive biparental care.
careNotes: The Bolivian Ram is generally considered one of the more forgiving dwarf cichlids, tolerating a wider temperature and pH range than its close relative Mikrogeophagus ramirezi (the German Blue Ram) and tending to be less prone to stress-related illness. It is best kept as a bonded pair in a well-established, mature aquarium, as individuals have been known to decline in new or biologically immature tanks. Pairs form strong bonds and exhibit biparental substrate-spawning behaviour, guarding eggs and fry on flat rocks, driftwood, broad plant leaves, or pits dug into the substrate. Some sources recommend starting with a small group of 6–8 individuals in a larger tank (120 cm or more) to allow natural pair formation, then rehoming any surplus fish once pairs have bonded. Males grow noticeably larger and develop more extended fins and deeper coloration than females. Adding open-water schooling fish such as tetras or rasboras — sometimes called dither fish — is commonly recommended to help reduce shyness in the rams. The species has been reported susceptible to head and lateral line erosion (HLLE), a condition marked by pitting or discoloration along the head and lateral line, under poor water conditions. See also [german-blue-ram] for comparison with the sibling species.
breedingNotes: Biparental substrate spawner. Pairs generally spawn without much coaxing once well conditioned on varied live/frozen foods and maintained in stable, clean water. Eggs are typically laid on a flat rock, piece of driftwood, broad plant leaf, or a shallow pit dug by the pair. Incubation has been reported at approximately 2–3 days; fry become free-swimming roughly 5–8 days after hatching. Both parents actively guard eggs and wriggler fry and have been known to move the eggs or wrigglers if disturbed. Fry can be started on microworms or baby brine shrimp nauplii. Clutch sizes have been reported around 100–150 eggs.

additional.name: SeriouslyFish
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (70–80 mm SL), water parameters, native range, tank footprint (90×45 cm minimum), and breeding behaviour. Recommends groups of 6–8 in 120 cm+ tanks; notes HLLE susceptibility.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (~3.5 in males = ~8.9 cm total length), lifespan (~4 years), min tank (30 gal = ~113 L), temperature (74–78°F = 23.3–25.6°C), pH (6.0–7.4), gH (6–14 dGH), diet, and compatibility. Minor disagreement on care level (rated beginner; primary/SeriouslyFish rate intermediate). Retained intermediate as it better reflects water-quality sensitivity and new-tank incompatibility documented by SeriouslyFish.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed family Cichlidae, order Cichliformes, native range (Guaporé and Mamoré drainages, Bolivia/Brazil). FishBase adult size 5.6 cm SL consistent with SeriouslyFish 70–80 mm SL range for smaller individuals; total length ~7–9 cm aligns with hobby sources.

fish.breedingNotes: The Bolivian Ram is a biparental substrate spawner that generally bonds and spawns without special intervention once well conditioned. Fry are manageable on standard micro-foods. Rehoming surplus fish from group setups can be a practical challenge once pairings have formed.
